Artificial Algae Algorithm

A bio-inspired metaheuristic algorithm for nonlinear global optimization

Original Publication

2015 · Applied Soft Computing

Artificial algae algorithm (AAA) for nonlinear global optimization

Sait Ali Uymaz, Gulay Tezel, Esra Yel

Volume
31
Pages
153-171
DOI
10.1016/j.asoc.2015.03.003
View on ScienceDirect ↗

Overview

Proposed in Applied Soft Computing in 2015, AAA is a population-based approach to nonlinear global optimization. It is inspired by selected living behaviours of microalgae, modelling colonies as candidate solutions that move, grow, reproduce, and adapt during the search process.

Biological Inspiration

Microalgae move toward light to support photosynthesis and can exhibit helical swimming behaviour. Under suitable light and nutrient conditions they grow and reproduce, while adapting to changing environmental conditions.

AAA translates these selected behaviours into computational mechanisms for exploring a search space and improving candidate solutions.

Main Components

Artificial algae colonies represent candidate solutions. Helical movement, evolutionary change, and adaptation combine to balance exploration and exploitation through fitness-based behaviour.

Helical Movement

Colonies move toward a light source through energy-dependent helical movement. Shear force and friction surface influence the movement, supporting a balance between exploration and exploitation.

Evolutionary Process

Colony growth is related to fitness and nutrient availability, with inspiration from the Monod model. Larger colonies represent better solutions, and the smallest colony may receive replicated cells from the largest colony.

Adaptation Process

Starvation-based adaptation allows insufficiently grown colonies to try to resemble the largest colony. An adaptation parameter controls this process.

Algorithm Flow

  1. Determine parameters: shear force, energy loss, and adaptation parameter.
  2. Initialize artificial algae colonies.
  3. Evaluate fitness and colony sizes.
  4. Perform helical movement while energy remains.
  5. Update starvation values.
  6. Apply the evolutionary process.
  7. Apply the adaptation process.
  8. Keep the best colony.
  9. Check the stopping criterion.
  10. Return the optimum solution.

Pseudocode

Set shear force, energy loss, and adaptation parameter
Initialize artificial algae colonies
Evaluate fitness and determine colony sizes
while stopping criterion is not met:
    perform helical movement while energy remains
    update starvation values
    apply evolutionary process
    apply adaptation process
    preserve the best colony found
return the optimum solution

Benchmarking and Validation

  • CEC'05 benchmark function set
  • Unimodal, multimodal, and hybrid functions
  • 10, 30, and 50 dimensions
  • 30 independent runs
  • Comparison with ABC, BA, DE, ACOR, and HSPOP
  • Pressure vessel design optimization compared with ABC and SPSO2011

Applications

  • Nonlinear global optimization
  • Numerical optimization
  • Engineering design optimization
  • Constrained optimization
  • Pressure vessel design optimization
  • Feature selection
  • Machine learning parameter optimization
  • Scheduling and decision support problems

Implementations

MATLAB and Python implementation materials will be provided through the separate public repository.

AAA CodeAAA CitationsHow to Cite AAA

Citation

Please cite the original AAA publication when using the method in research. The article PDF should not be redistributed here unless copyright permissions allow it; use the DOI or ScienceDirect link instead.
